one_line_description: Market-leading cloud CRM platform for sales, service, and marketing, with an extensible app platform (objects, flows, Apex).
auth_summary: Salesforce REST/GraphQL APIs authorize via OAuth 2.0 through External Client Apps (recommended) or Connected Apps (legacy; new creation restricted as of Spring '26). Multiple OAuth 2.0 flows are offered; token is sent as a Bearer header.
access_notes: Developers can sign up for a free Developer Edition org, which has API access enabled by default, and create an External Client App / Connected App to get OAuth credentials. However, customer orgs need Enterprise, Performance, Unlimited, or Developer Edition; Professional Edition requires a paid API add-on, and users need the 'API Enabled' permission.
api_surface_summary: Very broad surface: REST API over all standard and custom objects (CRUD, SOQL query, search, describe), plus GraphQL API, Bulk, Metadata, and Connect REST APIs covering essentially the whole platform.
main_blocker: End-user orgs must be on API-enabled editions (Enterprise+/Developer; Professional needs a paid add-on), and legacy Connected App creation is being restricted in favor of External Client Apps.
agent_notes: Official REST API docs confirm OAuth 2.0 only (via external client apps / connected apps); Salesforce offers several OAuth 2.0 flows (including a JWT bearer flow, which is still OAuth2). Notable shift: connected app creation is restricted as of Spring '26 in favor of External Client Apps, which any Composio integration setup docs should reflect. Salesforce also ships official MCP support broadly (DX MCP server on GitHub, plus Salesforce-hosted MCP servers GA for Enterprise Edition and above).

one_line_description: All-in-one CRM platform for marketing, sales, service, and content, with a free tier and extensive automation features.
auth_summary: HubSpot APIs support exactly two methods: OAuth 2.0 (required for public/multi-account apps and Marketplace listings) and private app access tokens for single-account integrations; both are sent as Authorization: Bearer headers. Legacy API keys were retired in 2022.
access_notes: Fully self-serve: create a free HubSpot CRM account and generate a private app access token in settings, or create a free developer account to register an OAuth app. No paid plan or approval needed for basic API access, though some endpoints require paid Hub tiers.
api_surface_summary: Broad REST API covering CRM objects (contacts, companies, deals, tickets, custom objects), engagements/activities, CRM search, marketing, CMS, conversations, and more; now on a date-based versioning scheme (e.g., /crm/objects/2026-03/contacts).
agent_notes: Cleanest case of the two: self-serve free account, two well-documented auth methods, official remote MCP server (OAuth with PKCE) plus a local developer MCP server. Caveats: OAuth access tokens are short-lived (~30 min, refresh-token based), rate limits vary by plan, and some API areas (e.g., certain marketing/CMS features) are gated behind paid Hub tiers. HubSpot is migrating API reference to a new date-based versioning scheme, so endpoint paths in older docs differ from the latest reference.

one_line_description: Sales-focused CRM for managing deals, pipelines, contacts, and sales activities.
auth_summary: Official docs confirm two methods: OAuth 2.0 (authorization code flow with scopes; client_id/client_secret issued when you create an app in Developer Hub) and a per-user API token passed in the x-api-token header. The API token is tied to one user/company and only one can be active at a time.
access_notes: Free developer sandbox account is self-serve; Developer Hub lets you create a draft app and immediately get client_id/client_secret. API tokens are available to any Pipedrive user from account settings. Public Marketplace listing requires app review, but private/unlisted apps and direct API-token integrations do not.
api_surface_summary: Mature REST API (v1 plus newer v2 endpoints) covering deals, leads, persons, organizations, activities, products, pipelines/stages, notes, files, users, custom fields, and webhooks.
agent_notes: Fetched official auth docs directly. Pipedrive launched a native (official) MCP server on June 30, 2026, available on all plans via OAuth. Only caveat for Composio: a public Marketplace-listed OAuth app must pass Pipedrive's review process, though private apps and API-token access skip it. Composio already lists a Pipedrive toolkit.

one_line_description: Modern, data-model-flexible CRM for managing companies, people, deals, and custom objects.
auth_summary: Attio's REST API accepts a workspace API key (generated in Workspace Settings > Developers) or an OAuth 2.0 authorization-code flow for third-party apps; either credential is sent as an Authorization: Bearer token on every request.
access_notes: Any workspace admin can self-serve an API key from Workspace Settings (free plan available); OAuth apps are registered by developers and documented with a public token endpoint and OAuth tutorial — no partner program or sales contact found.
api_surface_summary: Public REST API (JSON over HTTPS) covering the full CRM data model: objects (standard and custom), records, lists/entries, attributes, notes, tasks, comments, webhooks, and workspace members.
agent_notes: Fetched the official REST API overview (confirms public REST API and an OAuth app tutorial); the dedicated authentication page URL I guessed 404'd, so API-key and bearer-token details come from search results citing official Attio docs/help-center pages rather than a page I fetched end-to-end. Attio also ships an official hosted MCP server (OAuth-based, reads auto-approved, writes confirmed). Community MCP servers (e.g. kesslerio/attio-mcp-server) exist as well.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Open-source, self-hostable CRM (Salesforce alternative) with fully customizable objects, available self-hosted or as a cloud service.
auth_summary: Both REST and GraphQL APIs authenticate with a workspace-generated API key sent as 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY'. Keys are created self-serve in Settings > API & Webhooks and can be scoped to roles; no OAuth2 flow was found in official docs.
access_notes: Fully self-serve: self-host the open-source app (GitHub twentyhq/twenty) for free, or use Twenty Cloud; in either case any workspace user with settings access creates an API key in Settings > API & Webhooks.
api_surface_summary: Core API (CRUD on people, companies, opportunities, tasks, notes, and any custom objects, with filtering and relation traversal) plus a Metadata API for programmatically managing objects/fields/relations — both exposed over REST and GraphQL, with per-workspace generated schemas and an interactive playground.
agent_notes: Fetched official API docs directly. No official twentyhq MCP repo found — an open GitHub issue (#12953) requests one, and multiple community MCP servers exist (jezweb/twenty-mcp, mhenry3164/twenty-crm-mcp-server, IgorWarzocha/twenty-mcp-server); one search summary claimed Cloud workspaces ship a native MCP server but I could not verify that on docs.twenty.com, so I classified as community_mcp. Toolkit caveats: schema is per-workspace (custom objects mean dynamic tool generation works best), rate limit ~100 req/min, batches capped at 60 records, and self-hosted instances have per-instance base URLs.

one_line_description: Progress Podio is a customizable work-management/collaboration platform where teams build no-code apps to track leads, projects, and workflows.
auth_summary: The Podio API exclusively uses OAuth2 with four flows: server-side (authorization code), client-side (implicit), username/password grant, and an app-authentication flow for single-app access. Access tokens last 8 hours with 28-day refresh tokens, sent as 'Authorization: OAuth2 ACCESS_TOKEN'.
access_notes: Developers self-serve register an application (API key generator in Podio account settings) to get a Client ID and Client Secret; no partner program or approval process is documented, and Podio offers free/entry plans.
api_surface_summary: REST API split into roughly 40+ resource areas: items, applications, tasks, comments, files, hooks (webhooks), organizations, spaces, users, conversations, calendar, search, tags, forms, streams, and more.
agent_notes: Surprise: Podio now has an official remote MCP server at https://mcp.podio.com/mcp with 22 read/write tools (shared default credentials are limited to ~30-40 requests; own API key recommended). Product ownership moved Citrix -> Progress; despite past uncertainty, Progress's 2025 'Year in Review' confirms active investment and a 2026 roadmap, so platform-abandonment risk appears low. The password grant makes headless service auth easy but is discouraged in the docs.

one_line_description: Zoho CRM is a full-featured sales CRM for managing leads, contacts, deals, and sales automation across the Zoho suite.
auth_summary: Zoho CRM's API uses OAuth 2.0 exclusively; the docs explicitly note clients must not use password auth. Access tokens last one hour with long-lived refresh tokens; a 'Self Client' type supports backend-only apps without a redirect URL.
access_notes: Fully self-serve: register any of five client types (server-based, client-based, mobile, non-browser, self client) at the Zoho Developer Console (api-console.zoho.com) to get Client ID/Secret. A Zoho CRM account is needed; API call credits scale with the CRM edition, and Zoho operates multiple data centers with region-specific API domains.
api_surface_summary: v8 REST API spans Metadata APIs (modules, fields, layouts), Core CRUD APIs on all CRM modules, Composite API (batch up to 5 calls), asynchronous Bulk read/write APIs, Notification (webhook) APIs, and SQL-style Query (COQL) APIs.
agent_notes: Zoho ships official first-party MCP: a suite-wide Zoho MCP product plus four pre-built Zoho CRM MCP servers (OAuth flow on first tool invocation), supporting Claude, Cursor, VS Code, Windsurf. Integration caveats for a toolkit: multi-DC architecture means tokens/endpoints differ by region (.com, .eu, .in, etc.), and API rate limits are credit-based per edition (limits page not fetched directly - noted from general docs structure).

one_line_description: Close is a sales-focused CRM with built-in calling, email, and SMS for inside sales teams.
auth_summary: Close supports API keys ('for scripts') created self-serve under Settings > Developer > API Keys, and OAuth 2.0 for user-facing integrations (including OAuth 2.0 Dynamic Client Registration on its MCP server). The MCP server also accepts a Close-API-Key header with a Close-Scope header (mcp.read / mcp.write_safe / mcp.write_destructive).
access_notes: API keys are self-serve from account settings (Settings > Developer > API Keys), but Close is a paid product with a free trial and no permanent free tier; docs mention creating OAuth apps and getting listed, and direct some partner/API inquiries to support@close.com.
api_surface_summary: REST API covering leads, contacts, opportunities, activities (calls/emails/SMS), custom fields/objects, sequences, bulk email/SMS actions, webhooks, event logs, and reporting metrics.
agent_notes: Official remote MCP server at https://mcp.close.com/mcp (HTTP Streamable, OAuth 2.0 DCR, officially supported in the Claude ecosystem) with tiered write scopes - a strong signal for agent-readiness. One gap: docs I fetched did not state the exact wire format for API keys on the core REST API (widely known to be HTTP Basic auth with the key as username, but I could not confirm from an official page I saw, so Basic auth is not listed as a distinct method). Plan-level API access restrictions were not specified in fetched docs.

one_line_description: CRM (formerly ProsperWorks) built around Google Workspace, managing leads, contacts, companies, opportunities, and pipelines directly from Gmail.
auth_summary: API keys are self-generated in the Copper web app (System settings > API Keys) and sent as X-PW-AccessToken + X-PW-UserEmail headers on every request. OAuth2 Authorization Code flow is supported for partner integrations, but registering an OAuth client requires emailing partners@copper.com — it is not self-serve.
access_notes: API keys are self-serve inside any Copper account, but the Web API is only available on trial, Professional, and Business plans (not Starter/Basic). A free 14-day trial grants API access; OAuth client_id/client_secret requires contacting Copper's partner team (partners@copper.com).
api_surface_summary: REST API (v1, JSON) at api.copper.com/developer_api/v1 covering leads (incl. bulk ops and conversion), people, companies, opportunities, projects, tasks, activities, tags, custom fields, field layouts, related items, file upload, users/account, and webhooks.
main_blocker: OAuth2 app registration is gated behind emailing partners@copper.com, and API access requires a Professional+ (or trial) plan; API-key auth itself is fully self-serve.
agent_notes: Fetched official developer docs directly. Copper's unusual header-pair auth (X-PW-AccessToken + X-PW-UserEmail) reflects its ProsperWorks legacy. No official Copper MCP found; multiple community/aggregator MCPs exist (Apideck, Pipedream, viaSocket, n8n, SyncHub). Rate limits not documented on the pages fetched. Note developer.copper.co (different TLD) is an unrelated crypto company — easy to confuse.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Intapp DealCloud is a deal and relationship management platform (CRM + pipeline + relationship intelligence) for private equity, investment banking, and other financial services firms.
auth_summary: OAuth2 Client Credentials flow: a user's API key (enabled in their DealCloud profile after an admin grants the 'API' site-area capability) is exchanged along with client_id and scopes at the site's token endpoint for a bearer access token that expires after 900 seconds.
access_notes: API keys are generated inside a customer's DealCloud site (Admin enables API capability for the user group, then the user enables an API key in their profile). There is no self-serve signup or free developer sandbox — DealCloud is an enterprise contact-sales product (reported ~$85k+/yr), and sandboxes are purchased through an Intapp representative.
api_surface_summary: REST APIs covering authentication, user management, schema/metadata discovery, data CRUD (entries, rows, files, reports), backups/exports, publications (real-time event streaming), and relationship-intelligence data import; an official Python SDK is also documented.
main_blocker: Credentials only exist inside a paid customer DealCloud instance; no self-serve developer account or public sandbox, so Composio would need a customer/partner relationship with Intapp to build and test.
agent_notes: Official API docs (api.docs.dealcloud.com) are public and detailed, confirming auth flow and REST surface; the blocker is purely access, not documentation. Each customer site has its own domain-specific token endpoint. Pricing figures (~$85k-$1.4M/yr) come from third-party sources (Vendr/G2), not Intapp. No official or community MCP server found in searches or the MCP registry results. The toolkit design would need to handle per-tenant schemas since DealCloud data models are heavily customized per firm.

one_line_description: Market-leading customer service platform with ticketing, help center, chat, and voice support.
auth_summary: Supports API tokens (auto-generated in Admin Center, used via basic auth as {email}/token:{api_token}), basic auth with password, and OAuth2 access tokens (Bearer); global OAuth clients are required for apps distributed to multiple Zendesk customers.
access_notes: Self-serve trial signup; API tokens are generated in Admin Center (Apps and integrations > APIs > Zendesk API). OAuth clients can be created per-instance; global OAuth for multi-customer apps requires registration with Zendesk.
api_surface_summary: Very broad REST API covering ticketing (tickets, users, organizations, macros, triggers, views), Help Center, Talk, Chat, and Sell, with hundreds of documented endpoints.
agent_notes: Zendesk announced official MCP Client (early access) and an official MCP Server slated for early access in summer 2026, so official MCP may be imminent but is not GA; multiple community MCP servers exist today. Global OAuth registration adds friction for a multi-tenant Composio integration but per-instance OAuth and API tokens are fully self-serve.

one_line_description: Customer messaging and support platform with shared inbox, help center, and the Fin AI agent.
auth_summary: Access Tokens (sent as Authorization: Bearer) for private apps against your own workspace, and OAuth2 for public apps accessing other users' Intercom accounts.
access_notes: Developers create an app in the Developer Hub and automatically receive an Access Token (Configure > Authentication); free development workspaces support building and OAuth testing.
api_surface_summary: Broad REST API covering contacts, companies, conversations, tickets, articles/help center, admins, teams, tags, segments, events, and messages.
agent_notes: Official hosted remote MCP server exists (OAuth or bearer token auth) but currently only supports US-hosted workspaces (EU/AU unsupported). Public OAuth apps go through Intercom's app review only if listed on the App Store; unlisted OAuth apps are possible.

one_line_description: Freshworks' cloud helpdesk for ticketing, knowledge base, and customer support automation.
auth_summary: API key used via HTTP Basic Auth (apikey:X, base64-encoded); every user can copy their personal API key from Profile Settings. No OAuth2 for the core Freshdesk API.
access_notes: Self-serve signup (free trial/free tier); the personal API key is available immediately from the user's Profile Settings page in the support portal.
api_surface_summary: REST API v2 covering tickets, conversations, contacts, companies, agents, groups, roles, solutions (KB), surveys, time entries, automations, SLA policies, and custom objects.
agent_notes: Official Freshworks MCP server exists but is in beta/Early Access Program limited to selected Enterprise-plan customers and authenticates via API key only; multiple community MCP servers (e.g. NeuraLegion, Enreign, effytech) are freely available. API-key-only auth means per-user credential entry rather than OAuth connect flow.

one_line_description: Shared inbox and customer operations platform combining email, chat, and team collaboration.
auth_summary: API tokens generated from Front settings (sent as Authorization: Bearer) for single-instance integrations, plus OAuth2 which is required for public integrations distributed to all Front customers unless Front grants an exception.
access_notes: Free self-serve developer account at dev.frontapp.com provides a sandbox environment for building and testing; API tokens are generated from account settings. Publishing to the Front App Store requires building in a developer account and going through Front's partner/review process.
api_surface_summary: Core REST API covers conversations, messages, contacts, accounts, inboxes, channels, tags, comments, teammates, rules, and analytics.
agent_notes: No official MCP; Front staff acknowledged longer-term interest on their community forum but have not prioritized it. OAuth app distribution to all customers is tied to Front's app/partner review, though building and testing OAuth apps in a free developer sandbox is self-serve.

one_line_description: B2B customer support platform unifying Slack, email, and in-app support with account management.
auth_summary: API tokens created in-app; only Admin users can create API tokens. Public docs confirm token-based access but do not publicly detail the header format; the official MCP server separately uses OAuth 2.0 (AuthKit).
access_notes: No free trial or free plan; all tiers are annual contracts starting ~$59/seat/month (3-seat minimum), typically sold via demo. Once a customer, an admin can self-serve create API tokens.
api_surface_summary: REST API covering issues, accounts, contacts, messages, attachments, knowledge base, macros, tags, tasks/projects, teams, users/roles, custom fields/objects, surveys, and audit logs.
main_blocker: No self-serve or trial account: getting API credentials requires becoming a paying Pylon customer via a sales-led annual contract.
agent_notes: API reference index confirms broad resource coverage, but exact auth header format was not visible in the fetched docs pages (docs use a dynamic query system). Official hosted MCP server exists at mcp.usepylon.com with OAuth. Pricing/trial details came partly from third-party pricing roundups; Pylon reportedly offers occasional limited trial access on request.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Helpdesk software with multichannel ticketing, live chat, and call center features.
auth_summary: API v3 authenticates via an API key passed in an 'apikey' header; admins create keys (with per-key permissions) under Configuration > System > API. No OAuth2 found.
access_notes: Self-serve free trial signup; admins generate API keys directly from the agent panel. Full interactive API docs are hosted per-account at YOURDOMAIN.ladesk.com/docs/api/v3/.
api_surface_summary: REST API v3 covering tickets, chats, calls, agents, contacts, companies, departments, tags, and filters. Cloud accounts are rate-limited to 180 requests/minute per API key.
agent_notes: Full endpoint reference is only browsable inside a LiveAgent account domain (docs are per-instance), so breadth was assessed from support articles and the Python client repo rather than a public API reference page. No official or community MCP server found in searches.

one_line_description: API-first B2B support platform built for technical teams, with Slack-native support workflows.
auth_summary: API keys created via Machine Users (Settings > Machine Users) with fine-grained permissions, sent as Authorization: Bearer to the GraphQL endpoint. The official MCP server separately uses OAuth (no API keys needed).
access_notes: Self-serve workspace signup; API keys are generated in-app from Settings > API Keys / Machine Users with selectable permissions.
api_surface_summary: GraphQL API (core-api.uk.plain.com/graphql/v1) covering threads, customers, tenants, labels, events, and help center content; the official MCP exposes 30 tools across threads, customers, tenants, help center, and workspace.
agent_notes: GraphQL-only API (no REST), which is a minor implementation difference for a toolkit. Official hosted MCP at mcp.plain.com/mcp with OAuth; community MCP servers using API keys also exist. Did not independently verify pricing/trial specifics, but signup and API key creation are documented as in-app self-serve.

one_line_description: Email-based shared inbox and knowledge base platform for customer support teams.
auth_summary: Mailbox API 2.0 is OAuth2-only: Authorization Code flow for integrations used by other Help Scout users, and Client Credentials flow for internal integrations; all requests use a Bearer access token tied to an active user.
access_notes: Self-serve: any user creates an OAuth2 app under Your Profile > My Apps ('Create My App'); account signup is self-serve with a free trial/free tier.
api_surface_summary: Mailbox API 2.0 (REST) covers conversations, threads, customers, mailboxes/inboxes, users, teams, tags, workflows, and reports; a separate Docs API covers knowledge base content.
agent_notes: No official Help Scout MCP found; community servers (drewburchfield/help-scout-mcp-server) and aggregator MCPs (StackOne, 36 actions) exist. Note access token lengths change over time per docs, and all credentials must map to an active invited user in the account.

one_line_description: Ecommerce-focused helpdesk (deep Shopify integration) for support tickets across email, chat, and social.
auth_summary: Private apps authenticate with an API key plus the user's email via HTTP basic auth; OAuth2 is mandatory for public apps distributed through the Gorgias app store.
access_notes: API keys are obtained self-serve from a Gorgias account's settings (a paid plan or trial account is required); OAuth2 credentials require going through the app-store setup process.
api_surface_summary: REST API covering tickets, ticket messages, customers, tags, macros, teams, users, integrations, events, satisfaction surveys, and account settings.
agent_notes: Official first-party hosted MCP at mcp.gorgias.com/mcp, available on any paid Gorgias plan. Composio already lists a Gorgias toolkit (composio.dev/toolkits/gorgias appeared in search results). Public OAuth2 apps require the app-store setup process, a mild review-gate for a distributed OAuth integration.

one_line_description: Enterprise customer service platform organized around lifelong customer conversations rather than tickets.
auth_summary: Token-based HTTP Basic Authentication: API tokens are tied to a designated Gladly user with the 'API User' permission and passed in the Authorization header; a token can perform any API request without restriction. No OAuth2 found.
access_notes: Gladly is an enterprise, sales-led product with no self-serve signup; API tokens are generated in-app by users holding the API User permission, so credentials require an existing customer account.
api_surface_summary: REST API at developer.gladly.com/rest covering customers, conversations, agents, tasks, payloads/exports, jobs, and webhooks for the Gladly support platform.
main_blocker: Credentials require a paid, sales-led Gladly customer account; no developer sandbox or self-serve signup found, and auth is basic-auth tokens rather than OAuth.
agent_notes: Auth details came from official Gladly help/developer docs surfaced in search results rather than a full fetch of the API reference, so endpoint-level breadth is an estimate. No official or community MCP server found. API tokens are all-or-nothing (no scoping), which is a governance caveat for agent use.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Team messaging and collaboration platform with channels, DMs, huddles, and a large app/bot ecosystem.
auth_summary: Slack apps authenticate via OAuth 2.0, which issues bot tokens (xoxb), user tokens (xoxp), and app-level tokens; scopes are assigned per app and token rotation is supported.
access_notes: Developers create an app self-serve at api.slack.com/apps, assign OAuth scopes, and install to a workspace to obtain tokens. Some enterprise workspaces require admin approval to install apps.
api_surface_summary: The Web API (HTTP methods like chat.postMessage, conversations.*, users.*) covers messaging, channels, users, files, canvases, plus Events API and Socket Mode for real-time events.
agent_notes: Fetched official auth docs. Slack now also ships an official MCP server documented on docs.slack.dev. Composio-style toolkits are well-trodden here; main caveat is per-workspace app installation and admin approval in restricted workspaces.

one_line_description: CPaaS platform for programmable SMS, voice, WhatsApp, email, and verification via APIs.
auth_summary: REST APIs authenticate with HTTP Basic auth using Account SID + Auth Token, or (preferred for production) API keys (Main/Standard/Restricted) used as Basic auth credentials.
access_notes: Self-serve signup with a free trial account; trial has restrictions (verified numbers, trial message watermark). Full sending requires funding the account and, for some channels, regulatory/sender registration (e.g. A2P 10DLC).
api_surface_summary: REST APIs over HTTPS covering messaging, voice calls, phone number lookup/provisioning, verification, and more, with official SDKs in many languages.
agent_notes: Official docs confirm API keys are the preferred auth. Twilio Labs publishes an official (alpha) MCP server exposing 1,400+ endpoints generated from OpenAPI specs. Carrier/regulatory registration affects real-world SMS sending but not API buildability.

one_line_description: Zoho's team chat app with channels, bots, and workflow integrations, part of the Zoho suite.
auth_summary: The Cliq REST API v2 uses Zoho's standard OAuth 2.0 (register a client in the Zoho API Console, obtain access/refresh tokens with Cliq scopes).
access_notes: Developers register an application self-serve in the Zoho API Console to get client ID/secret; a free Cliq plan exists, so credentials are obtainable without payment. Regional data centers use different token/API endpoints.
api_surface_summary: REST API v2 at cliq.zoho.com/api/v2 covers chats, messages, channels, threads, users, roles, departments, teams, calendars/events, bots, reminders, and file management.
agent_notes: Fetched official v2 REST docs confirming OAuth2 and a wide endpoint list. Zoho hosts official MCP servers (mcp.zoho.com) that include Cliq. Multi-DC (zoho.com/.in/.eu) endpoints add minor integration complexity.

one_line_description: ByteDance's collaboration suite (messaging, docs, calendar, video) with an extensive open platform for apps and bots.
auth_summary: Server APIs use bearer access tokens: tenant_access_token/app_access_token obtained with the app's App ID + App Secret, and user_access_token obtained via an OAuth/OIDC user authorization flow. Tokens expire and must be refreshed.
access_notes: Developers create a custom app self-serve in the Lark Developer Console to get App ID/App Secret; API scopes must be requested per app and some require tenant admin approval before use in an organization.
api_surface_summary: Very broad OpenAPI surface covering messaging (IM), contacts, calendar, docs/sheets/base, approvals, and more, plus events/callbacks; documented at open.larksuite.com.
agent_notes: Auth model confirmed from official open.larksuite.com doc pages via search snippets rather than full page fetches. Official MCP exists (larksuite/lark-openapi-mcp on GitHub). Note Feishu (CN) vs Lark (international) have separate consoles/endpoints; scope approval by tenant admins is a mild operational caveat.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Free team chat app (by CAKE.com) with channels, threads, and calls, positioned as a Slack alternative.
auth_summary: API access is via an API-keys addon: after installing the addon in a workspace, users generate personal API keys with the /api-keys generate command and pass them to the HTTP API.
access_notes: Free self-serve: sign up for Pumble, install the API addon from the addon marketplace, then generate API keys in-app. No OAuth app registration flow documented for the public API.
api_surface_summary: Swagger-documented HTTP API for sending messages, replies, reactions, and listing/managing channels and users; rate limited to ~1000 requests/min per user. There is also a pumble-sdk for building bot-style apps.
main_blocker: Narrow addon-based API with per-user API keys and no OAuth app model
agent_notes: API is delivered as a marketplace addon rather than a first-class platform API; docs are thin. Only community MCP listings found (e.g. shoutkol pumble-mcp-server on LobeHub); no official Pumble MCP. Auth is API-key only per evidence found.

one_line_description: Community chat platform with servers, channels, voice, and a massive bot ecosystem.
auth_summary: The API accepts an Authorization header with either a bot token ('Bot <token>' from the app's Bot page) or an OAuth2 bearer token ('Bearer <token>') for user-delegated access.
access_notes: Register an application self-serve in the Discord Developer Portal; bot token is issued immediately. Privileged gateway intents (message content, presence, members) and bots in 100+ servers require verification/approval.
api_surface_summary: HTTP REST API (discord.com/api) covering messages, channels, guilds, members, roles, threads, slash commands, plus a Gateway WebSocket API for real-time events.
agent_notes: Fetched official API reference (now hosted at docs.discord.com). Many community MCP servers exist; no official Discord MCP found. Caveat for a toolkit: message-content privileged intent and bot verification at scale, and the bot must be invited to each guild.

one_line_description: Global messaging app whose Bot API lets developers build bots that send/receive messages, manage chats, and take payments.
auth_summary: The Bot API authenticates with a bot token embedded in the URL (https://api.telegram.org/bot<token>/METHOD), issued free by @BotFather. Full user-account access requires the separate MTProto API with api_id/api_hash (not standard REST auth).
access_notes: Completely self-serve and free: create a bot via BotFather in the Telegram app and receive the token instantly. MTProto api_id/api_hash are also self-serve via my.telegram.org.
api_surface_summary: HTTP-based Bot API covers sending/receiving all message types, inline queries, chat/member administration, polls, payments, and web apps; limited to what bots can see (no arbitrary user-account history without MTProto).
agent_notes: Fetched official Bot API docs. Many community MCP servers exist (both Bot API-based and MTProto/Telethon user-account based); none official from Telegram. A Composio toolkit would realistically be bot-scoped: bots only see chats they are added to.

one_line_description: Meta's Cloud API for sending and receiving WhatsApp messages programmatically as a business.
auth_summary: Cloud API calls (Meta Graph API) use bearer access tokens: temporary developer tokens for testing and permanent System User access tokens (with whatsapp_business_messaging / whatsapp_business_management permissions) for production. Not a standard user-facing OAuth2 flow for messaging.
access_notes: Self-serve: register as a Meta developer, create an app with the WhatsApp use case, and get a test number + temporary token immediately. Production sending at scale requires a real phone number, system-user token, and business verification/messaging-limit tiers.
api_surface_summary: Graph API endpoints for sending template and free-form messages (within the 24-hour customer service window), media, message templates, phone number and WABA management, plus webhooks for inbound messages and statuses.
main_blocker: Business verification and template/24-hour-window messaging rules gate production use
agent_notes: Fetched official get-started docs. Most popular community MCPs (e.g. lharries/whatsapp-mcp) bridge the unofficial WhatsApp Web protocol rather than the Business Cloud API; a Composio toolkit should target the official Cloud API with system-user tokens. Testing is free/self-serve, but real-world messaging requires verified business + approved templates.

one_line_description: Cloud business phone system for sales/support teams with calls, SMS/WhatsApp, and CRM integrations.
auth_summary: Customers use HTTP Basic auth with an api_id/api_token pair generated in the Aircall Dashboard; technology partners use OAuth 2.0 with client_id/client_secret issued after signing up to Aircall's partner program.
access_notes: API keys are generated in the company dashboard, so you need an Aircall account (paid subscription; short free trial available). OAuth credentials require applying via aircall.io/partners/technology/signup and being onboarded by Aircall.
api_surface_summary: REST API covering users, teams, numbers, calls (list/transfer/tag/archive), contacts, SMS/messaging, call AI features (transcripts, sentiment, summaries), webhooks, and analytics.
main_blocker: Credentials require a paid Aircall subscription (Basic auth) or partner-program approval (OAuth2)
agent_notes: Fetched official API reference confirming both auth modes. The well-known Aircall MCP server (73 tools) is explicitly unofficial, built by a competitor (Allo/The Mobile First Company). API itself is well documented; the gate is account/partner access, not technology.

one_line_description: CPaaS (formerly Nexmo) offering SMS, voice, video, verify, and network APIs.
auth_summary: APIs authenticate either with the account API key + secret (Base64-encoded in a Basic Authorization header, or as query/body params on older APIs) or with JWTs signed by a Vonage Application's private key; which method applies varies per API (e.g. Verify v2 and Voice favor JWT).
access_notes: Self-serve signup on the Vonage dashboard issues an API key and secret immediately, with free trial credit; applications (for JWT auth) are also created self-serve in the dashboard. Some network APIs require additional enablement.
api_surface_summary: REST APIs for SMS, Messages (WhatsApp/Viber/MMS), Voice, Verify, Number Insight, Numbers provisioning, Video, and newer network APIs, with SDKs across major languages.
agent_notes: The specific auth doc URL I tried 404'd, but multiple official developer.vonage.com pages surfaced in search confirm API key/secret Basic auth plus application JWT auth. Vonage publishes official MCP servers (documentation MCP under the Vonage org; server/tooling MCPs announced on Postman) and community telephony MCP under Vonage-Community. Per-API auth-method variation is the main integration nuance.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Google's advertising platform for search, display, YouTube and shopping campaigns, managed programmatically via the Google Ads API.
auth_summary: All API calls require OAuth2 (user consent flow or service account/domain-wide delegation with scope https://www.googleapis.com/auth/adwords) PLUS a Google Ads developer token attached to a manager (MCC) account. OAuth alone is not sufficient.
access_notes: Developer token is issued instantly but starts at Test Account Access (test accounts only). Google may auto-upgrade to Explorer Access (limited production ops/day); Basic Access (15k ops/day) and Standard Access (unlimited) require an application form review with business justification and API policy compliance.
api_surface_summary: Full campaign lifecycle: accounts, campaigns, ad groups, ads, budgets, bidding, audiences, keywords, conversions, and reporting via GAQL query language. Available as REST and gRPC with official client libraries.
main_blocker: Developer token access-level approval (Basic/Standard) required before production accounts can be used at scale
agent_notes: Google published an official open-source MCP server (googleads/google-ads-mcp, verified by fetching the repo) — initial version is read-only/diagnostics-focused and still requires your own developer token (Explorer+ for production) and OAuth credentials. Many community MCPs also exist. The developer token + MCC requirement is the practical hurdle for any multi-tenant toolkit.

one_line_description: Meta's Marketing API for creating and managing Facebook/Instagram ad campaigns, audiences, creatives, and insights.
auth_summary: OAuth2 via Facebook Login producing user access tokens (short-lived, exchangeable for 60-day long-lived tokens) with ads_management/ads_read permissions; non-expiring system user tokens from Business Manager are the recommended production pattern. Tokens are sent as Bearer tokens to the Graph API.
access_notes: Creating a Meta developer app is self-serve and dev-mode works against your own ad accounts immediately. Serving external users requires Meta App Review for ads_management/business_management (Advanced Access, with business verification), and higher rate limits require the 'Marketing API Access Tier' feature (formerly Ads Management Standard Access), qualified via 500+ API calls in 15 days with <15% error rate.
api_surface_summary: Graph API-based REST surface covering campaigns, ad sets, ads, creatives, custom audiences, targeting, catalogs, and the Insights reporting API across Facebook and Instagram.
main_blocker: Meta App Review + Advanced Access (and business verification) required to act on ad accounts outside your own Business Manager
agent_notes: Meta launched official 'Meta Ads AI Connectors' — an MCP server in open beta requiring no developer credentials — plus popular community MCPs (e.g. pipeboard-co/meta-ads-mcp). AMSA was renamed 'Marketing API Access Tier' in May 2026 per Meta's developer blog; docs terminology may be in flux. Auth/access details confirmed via official doc snippets in search results but I did not fully fetch the authorization page.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: LinkedIn's Marketing/Advertising API for managing sponsored campaigns, audiences, and ad reporting on LinkedIn.
auth_summary: 3-legged OAuth2 (authorization code flow, member authorization) is required for Marketing APIs; you register a LinkedIn developer app and users grant it access. No API-key option.
access_notes: You must create a developer app and apply for the Advertising API product via an access-request form; LinkedIn reviews the use case (typically 2-4 weeks, generic data-collection use cases rejected). Access starts at a Development tier, and a further 'Increasing Access' review is needed for standard/production-scale access.
api_surface_summary: Versioned REST API covering ad accounts, campaign groups, campaigns, creatives, targeting/audiences (Matched Audiences), conversions, lead gen forms, and analytics/reporting.
main_blocker: Advertising API access is gated behind LinkedIn's application review with business justification; no self-serve production credentials
agent_notes: No official LinkedIn MCP found; several community MCPs exist (danielpopamd, CData read-only, Zapier-hosted). The gate is real but not a formal paid partner program — ads-management/reporting use cases reportedly do get approved, so an established integrator like Composio could likely obtain access, just not instantly.

one_line_description: All-in-one agency marketing/CRM platform (funnels, pipelines, calendars, SMS/email) with a v2 developer API and app marketplace.
auth_summary: API v2 supports OAuth2 authorization code flow for marketplace apps, and Private Integration Tokens (PIT) — static scoped bearer tokens created per sub-account/agency — for internal single-account integrations. Legacy v1 API keys are deprecated.
access_notes: Developer portal (marketplace.gohighlevel.com) signup is self-serve for creating OAuth apps; Private Integration Tokens are generated directly in a HighLevel account's settings. Testing requires a HighLevel account (paid SaaS, no free tier beyond trial), and public marketplace listing involves an app review.
api_surface_summary: v2 REST API covers contacts, conversations, opportunities/pipelines, calendars, campaigns/workflows, payments, custom fields/values, locations, and more, with webhooks for events.
agent_notes: HighLevel ships an official MCP server (authenticated with a Private Integration Token + location ID) exposing contacts, conversations, calendars, opportunities, payments. Note the official MCP is reportedly bundled free only on Unlimited/SaaS Pro plans. Docs now live at marketplace.gohighlevel.com/docs (the stoplight.io hint is the older doc host). Users need paid GHL accounts, but that's true of their customer base by definition.

one_line_description: Email marketing platform with APIs for audiences, campaigns, automations, and transactional email (Mandrill).
auth_methods: API key; OAuth2; Basic auth; Bearer token
auth_summary: Marketing API accepts a self-generated API key (sent via HTTP Basic or Bearer) for your own account, or OAuth2 authorization-code flow for accessing other users' accounts; OAuth access tokens do not expire. Requests are routed to a per-account server prefix (e.g. us19).
access_notes: Fully self-serve: create an API key in account settings, or register an OAuth app in the developer section. Free Mailchimp tier exists for testing. No approval process found.
api_surface_summary: Marketing API covers audiences/lists, members, segments, tags, campaigns, templates, automations, reports, and e-commerce; separate Transactional (Mandrill) API for transactional sending.
agent_notes: Mailchimp's official MCP (https://mandrillapp.com/mcp) covers only the Transactional Messaging (Mandrill) API, not the main Marketing API; Marketing API coverage exists via multiple community MCPs (e.g. damientilman/mailchimp-mcp-server with 112+ tools). Straightforward build.

one_line_description: Customer data and marketing automation platform for email/SMS, with rich APIs for profiles, events, campaigns, and flows.
auth_methods: Private app token; OAuth2; API key
auth_summary: Server-side APIs authenticate via a private API key (pk_-prefixed, sent as 'Authorization: Klaviyo-API-Key ...') with configurable scopes, or via OAuth2 bearer tokens (required for App Marketplace apps, better rate limits); a public API key/site ID is used only for client-side endpoints.
access_notes: Fully self-serve: generate private API keys in account settings (free Klaviyo tier available); OAuth apps are created via Klaviyo's developer portal. Marketplace listing has a review, but API credentials themselves are unrestricted.
api_surface_summary: Versioned JSON:API REST surface covering profiles, lists, segments, events/metrics, campaigns, flows, templates, catalogs, coupons, and reporting.
agent_notes: Klaviyo has a first-class official MCP server (GA since Aug 2025, local + remote variants) documented in its developer docs — one of the most mature official MCPs in this category. Klaviyo is actively steering marketplace apps toward OAuth over private keys.

one_line_description: All-in-one funnel builder with email marketing, courses, and automations, exposing a public API for contacts and related resources.
auth_summary: Public API authenticates with an API key passed in the X-API-Key header; keys are created self-serve in account settings (max 3 per account, with expiration dates). No OAuth found — the docs even solicit feedback on which auth methods to add next.
access_notes: Fully self-serve: any account (including the free plan) can create a public API key under Settings > Public API keys. No review or approval process.
api_surface_summary: REST API centered on contacts, tags, and contact fields (with cursor pagination and PATCH support); documentation is thin and the developer portal intro documents little beyond contacts/tags — funnels, courses, and payments coverage is unclear.
main_blocker: API surface is partial but broader than contacts/tags: official endpoint index lists contacts, tags, funnels, newsletters, payments/subscriptions, courses, communities, and webhooks
agent_notes: Surprise: systeme.io ships an official MCP server (contacts, tags, custom fields, newsletters, funnels per their help article) — its MCP appears to cover funnels/newsletters that the public API docs don't clearly document, suggesting docs lag behind capability. I fetched developer.systeme.io/reference/api directly: intro documents only contacts/tags and asks users which auth methods to implement next, so breadth is genuinely unclear. Confidence lowered accordingly.
verification_notes: main_blocker corrected in QA: The official endpoint index shows roughly ten resource groups (funnels, courses, communities, payments, webhooks, etc.), contradicting the 'mainly contacts/tags' characterization even though the surface is still partial.

one_line_description: Visual discovery platform whose API v5 covers pins, boards, ads, catalogs, and analytics.
auth_summary: API v5 uses OAuth2: authorization code flow (with refresh tokens) for user-context access to the full API, plus client credentials grant for app-own-behalf/machine-to-machine cases. No plain API key.
access_notes: Register an app on developers.pinterest.com, then request Trial Access (limited rate limits); full-scope Standard/production access requires an approval process including demonstrating a working interactive OAuth login flow to Pinterest.
api_surface_summary: REST API v5 covering pins, boards, user account data, ads (campaigns/ad groups/ads/reporting), audiences, catalogs/shopping, and conversions API.
main_blocker: Trial access has limited rate limits and standard access requires Pinterest's review/approval process
agent_notes: No official Pinterest MCP found — only community servers (some, like terryso/mcp-pinterest, scrape rather than use the API; collactivelabs' uses the real API). Pinterest maintains an official api-quickstart repo on GitHub. Trial-vs-standard access details came from official docs surfaced in search; I did not fetch the access-request page itself.

one_line_description: Meta's text-based social network, with a Threads API for publishing posts, managing replies, and reading insights.
auth_summary: OAuth2 authorization code flow against Threads-specific endpoints: short-lived (1h) user access tokens exchangeable for long-lived 60-day tokens that must be refreshed before expiry; tokens carry Threads permissions (e.g. threads_basic, threads_content_publish).
access_notes: Create a Meta developer app with the Threads use case — self-serve in development mode (limited test users). Publishing for arbitrary users in production requires Meta App Review with screencast demos per permission (typically a few business days).
api_surface_summary: Graph-API-style REST endpoints for creating/publishing threads (text, image, video, carousel), reply management/moderation, user profile info, and post/account insights. No ads or DM surface.
main_blocker: Meta App Review required for production use with external users; 60-day token lifetime requires refresh handling
agent_notes: No official Threads MCP from Meta found — only community servers. Note one prominent GitHub result (quinnjr/threads-mcp) targets 'Threads.com', so vet community options carefully. Auth/token lifecycle confirmed from official docs snippets; I did not fetch the docs pages directly (session limit hit on that fetch).

one_line_description: Twilio's email delivery platform (transactional + marketing email) with the widely used v3 Web API.
auth_summary: v3 API authenticates with self-generated API keys sent as 'Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>'; keys support scoped permissions (Full Access, Restricted/Custom, Billing). API keys replaced legacy username/password Basic auth.
access_notes: Sign up self-serve and create API keys immediately, but SendGrid discontinued its permanent free tier as of July 26, 2025 — new accounts get a 60-day free trial (100 emails/day), after which paid plans start around $19.95/month.
api_surface_summary: v3 REST API covers mail send, dynamic templates, contacts/lists/segments, marketing campaigns (Single Sends), suppressions, domain/sender authentication, webhooks, and statistics.
agent_notes: No official Twilio/SendGrid MCP found, though Twilio's own blog publishes a tutorial on building one; multiple community MCPs exist. Simplest auth model in this batch (single bearer API key). The 2025 removal of the permanent free tier only affects testing cost, not buildability.

one_line_description: Leading hosted ecommerce platform for building and running online stores.
auth_summary: OAuth2 (authorization code grant or token exchange) for public apps; custom apps get admin-generated Admin API access tokens installed per store. Static token generation outside the app model has been removed, so all new integrations go through app-based OAuth/token flows.
access_notes: Free Shopify Partner/dev accounts with development stores are self-serve; merchants can create custom apps in their own admin to generate Admin API access tokens without review. Public app store listing requires Shopify app review, but credentials for direct integration are self-serve.
api_surface_summary: GraphQL Admin API (now the primary API; REST Admin API is legacy) covers products, orders, customers, inventory, fulfillment, discounts, metafields, and more, plus Storefront API and webhooks.
agent_notes: Shopify ships official MCP servers (Storefront MCP on every store, Customer Accounts MCP, and a local Dev MCP). Note REST Admin API is deprecated for new public apps in favor of GraphQL, so a new toolkit should target GraphQL Admin API.

one_line_description: Open-source ecommerce plugin for WordPress with a built-in REST API per store.
auth_methods: Basic auth; API key; HMAC signature
auth_summary: Store admins generate consumer key/secret API keys (WooCommerce > Settings > Advanced > REST API) used as HTTP Basic auth credentials over HTTPS; over plain HTTP a one-legged OAuth 1.0a-style HMAC signature scheme is required. No standard OAuth2 flow.
access_notes: Fully self-serve, but per-store: each merchant generates their own consumer key/secret in wp-admin with read/write scope. There is no central developer portal or app review; the software itself is free and self-hosted.
api_surface_summary: REST API (v3) covers products, orders, customers, coupons, refunds, shipping, taxes, reports, settings, and webhooks.
agent_notes: WooCommerce now has native/official MCP integration documented in its developer docs (built on the WordPress Abilities system with a local proxy), plus many community MCP servers. Main operational caveat for Composio: credentials are per-store (each user must paste their own site URL + key/secret); connection quality depends on each store's hosting/SSL setup.

one_line_description: SaaS ecommerce platform for online stores with extensive management and storefront APIs.
auth_summary: All API accounts are OAuth2-based: single-click apps use the OAuth2 authorization code flow against login.bigcommerce.com to obtain a permanent access_token, while merchants can also self-create store-level API accounts that directly yield an access token sent via the X-Auth-Token header.
access_notes: Self-serve: developers get free sandbox stores via the BigCommerce developer program, and merchants create store-level API credentials (client id/secret + access token with chosen scopes) directly in the store control panel. Marketplace app listing requires review, but direct API access does not.
api_surface_summary: REST Management APIs plus a GraphQL Admin API and GraphQL Storefront API, covering catalog, orders, customers, carts/checkout, pricing, webhooks, and store settings.
agent_notes: BigCommerce announced an official Storefront MCP server (shopper-facing: catalog search, cart, checkout handoff); admin-side MCP coverage is community (e.g., isaacgounton/bigcommerce-api-mcp, StackOne). Store-level access tokens make per-user connection simple for Composio.

one_line_description: Enterprise B2C/B2B commerce platform (formerly Demandware) with the Salesforce Commerce API (SCAPI).
auth_summary: Admin SCAPI endpoints are secured via OAuth2 client credentials issued through Account Manager; Shopper APIs use SLAS (Shopper Login and API Access Service), which issues JWT shopper access tokens (~30 min validity) via OAuth2-style grant flows, including trusted-system tokens.
access_notes: There is no self-serve signup for a Commerce Cloud instance: you need a licensed B2C Commerce instance (enterprise contract) or partner-program sandbox access, then an admin with Account Manager roles must create API clients and SLAS clients for you. Legacy OCAPI also exists behind the same instance requirement.
api_surface_summary: SCAPI covers shopper flows (search, baskets, checkout, login) and admin resources (products, catalogs, orders, customers, pricing, promotions), alongside the older OCAPI REST API.
main_blocker: Credentials require a licensed B2C Commerce instance and Account Manager/SLAS admin setup; no self-serve developer sandbox.
agent_notes: Docs are public and detailed, but I could not confirm any self-serve path to an instance; access model is inferred from Account Manager role requirements and Salesforce's enterprise sales motion. Salesforce announced MCP support across Salesforce (June 2025), but Commerce Cloud-specific MCP servers found are community-built (sfcc-dev-mcp is docs/logs-focused; commercecloud-mcp-server uses OCAPI).
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Open-source (Magento Open Source) and commercial (Adobe Commerce) ecommerce platform with REST and GraphQL web APIs.
auth_summary: Self-hosted Magento/Adobe Commerce supports token-based auth (admin/customer bearer tokens) and admin-created 'Integrations' that generate consumer key/secret + access token pairs signed with OAuth 1.0a (not OAuth2). Adobe Commerce as a Cloud Service instead uses OAuth2 via Adobe IMS, including server-to-server flows.
access_notes: Anyone can run Magento Open Source for free and create integration/admin tokens in the admin panel self-serve; Adobe Commerce (paid product) works the same way per instance, while the newer Cloud Service requires an Adobe org and IMS-based OAuth2 credentials.
api_surface_summary: REST and GraphQL APIs cover catalog, carts/checkout, orders, customers, inventory, CMS, promotions, and admin configuration — several hundred REST routes.
agent_notes: Auth is fragmented across editions: legacy OAuth 1.0a integrations (not in the allowed enum; noted here), plain bearer admin tokens, and OAuth2/IMS only for the new Cloud Service. Like WooCommerce, connections are per-instance (user supplies their own base URL + token). Multiple community MCP servers exist (magento-mcp, magemcp, Codexpect, CData); no Adobe-official MCP found.

one_line_description: Website builder with ecommerce features and Commerce APIs for orders, products, and inventory.
auth_summary: Requests use a Bearer header carrying either a site-scoped API key (generated in site Settings > Advanced > Developer API Keys, non-expiring) or an OAuth2 access token (30-minute expiry, 7-day refresh tokens) for registered OAuth clients.
access_notes: API key generation requires the merchant site to be on the Commerce Advanced plan. OAuth client registration is not self-serve: you submit a form and Squarespace reviews the registration before issuing client_id/client_secret.
api_surface_summary: Commerce APIs cover orders, products, inventory, transactions, profiles, and webhooks; there is no comparable public API for general site content/pages.
main_blocker: API keys require the merchant's Commerce Advanced plan, and multi-tenant OAuth requires a reviewed client registration with short-lived tokens.
agent_notes: Buildable today via user-supplied API keys, but only for merchants on the top-tier commerce plan; the OAuth path requires Squarespace's manual review of the client registration. API surface is commerce-only and narrower than Shopify/BigCommerce. Only community MCP found (unofficial GitHub repo).

one_line_description: Embeddable ecommerce platform (Ecwid by Lightspeed) that adds a store to any website, with a REST API.
auth_summary: Public apps use OAuth2 (code exchanged via POST to my.ecwid.com/api/oauth/token) to obtain access tokens; every app also has non-expiring private (read/write) and public (read-only) access tokens, sent as Bearer tokens. Store owners integrating their own store can skip the OAuth flow and use the token directly.
access_notes: Developers register an app via the Ecwid developer portal and get tokens self-serve; installing on your own store yields an access token immediately. REST API access generally requires the store to be on a paid Ecwid plan.
api_surface_summary: REST API covers products, categories, orders, customers, discount coupons, store profile/settings, and webhooks.
agent_notes: Docs have migrated from api-docs.ecwid.com to docs.ecwid.com. Only community MCP presence found (a Pipedream-powered Ecwid MCP listed in an awesome-mcp-servers registry); no vendor MCP. The paid-plan requirement for API access is a common caveat I could not re-confirm from the current official docs in this pass — flagging as minor uncertainty.

one_line_description: Platform for creators to sell digital products, memberships, and licenses directly to audiences.
auth_summary: The v2 API uses OAuth2 (create an application, exchange code at api.gumroad.com/oauth/token) for multi-account apps; for your own account you can self-generate a non-expiring access token from the application settings and send it with each request.
access_notes: Fully self-serve: any Gumroad user can create an application in their account settings and generate an access token immediately; no review process for API access.
api_surface_summary: Seller-focused REST API covering products, sales, subscribers, offer codes, custom fields, license verification, and resource subscriptions (webhooks); no buyer/checkout-creation API.
agent_notes: Simple, stable, self-serve API but a small surface (mostly read/analytics plus license verification and webhooks). Multiple community MCP servers exist; no official Gumroad MCP found.

one_line_description: Amazon's SP-API for sellers and vendors to programmatically manage listings, orders, inventory, fulfillment, and reports on Amazon marketplaces.
auth_summary: Authorization is OAuth2 via Login with Amazon (LWA): registered applications obtain LWA access tokens (1-hour expiry) on behalf of authorizing selling partners; restricted operations returning PII additionally require Restricted Data Tokens. LWA client secrets rotate every 180 days.
access_notes: You must register as an SP-API developer (via a Professional seller/vendor account in Seller/Vendor Central) and have your developer profile approved before creating an app and getting LWA credentials; access to restricted/PII roles requires additional Amazon review, and public apps go through the Selling Partner Appstore authorization workflow.
api_surface_summary: Dozens of REST API sections: orders, listings, catalog, FBA inventory and fulfillment, feeds, reports, finances, shipping, notifications, Data Kiosk analytics, and more.
main_blocker: Developer registration and profile approval through a Professional selling account gate all credentials; PII-restricted roles need extra vetting.
agent_notes: Amazon has published official MCP tooling in its selling-partner-api-samples repo (sp-api-dev-mcp npm package and a Data Kiosk MCP server), though these are developer-tool/sample oriented rather than a hosted production MCP; several community/hosted SP-API MCP servers also exist. Note the older AWS SigV4 signing requirement has been dropped for most operations in favor of pure LWA tokens, but an AWS account is still referenced in parts of the docs.

one_line_description: Payments and monetization platform for creators and coaching/info businesses: checkouts, subscriptions, and payouts.
auth_summary: The FanBasis API reference (apidocs.fan) documents API-key-only authentication: every request must include the key in an x-api-key header, with separate sandbox and production keys. No OAuth or other flows are documented.
access_notes: Per the docs, developers copy their API key from the API Keys section of the FanBasis dashboard — self-serve once you have a FanBasis account (which itself involves onboarding as a creator/business on the platform). A second docs portal (dev-docs.fanbasis.com) is login-gated.
api_surface_summary: REST API plus webhooks covering checkout sessions, payments/transactions, subscriptions (renewals, cancellations), customers and saved payment methods, discounts, disputes, and refunds; ~13 webhook event types. A Node checkout SDK (@fanbasis/checkout-sdk) exists on npm.
main_blocker: Small, payments-focused API from a young vendor; primary developer portal is login-gated, and getting an account (and thus a key) requires being onboarded as a FanBasis merchant.
agent_notes: Contrary to the expectation of little/no public API, FanBasis does have a public REST API reference at apidocs.fan (API-key auth, payments/subscriptions/webhooks) and a Zapier integration. Confidence is medium because the main dev-docs.fanbasis.com portal is gated, the apidocs.fan domain's official status is inferred, and there is no way to verify credential issuance without a merchant account. No MCP server found anywhere.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Pay-as-you-go SEO data APIs covering SERPs, keywords, backlinks, on-page audits, and domain analytics.
auth_summary: Basic authentication only: API login (account email) plus an auto-generated API password, Base64-encoded in the Authorization header. Docs explicitly state Basic auth is the only way to access the API and credentials cannot be passed as URL parameters.
access_notes: Self-serve registration; API credentials (login + auto-generated API password) are issued with the account. Usage is billed via a pay-as-you-go credit balance rather than a subscription.
api_surface_summary: REST API v3 with many modules: SERP, Keywords Data, Backlinks, OnPage, DataForSEO Labs, Business Data, Domain Analytics, Content Analysis, AI Optimization, and Merchant.
agent_notes: Very clean target: single documented auth method, broad documented REST surface, official vendor MCP (npm dataforseo-mcp-server plus hosted endpoint mcp.dataforseo.com/mcp). Only real caveat is that all calls consume paid credits.

one_line_description: SEO platform (rank tracking, backlinks, audits, keyword research) with a Data API and a Project API.
auth_summary: API key created in the account's API Dashboard, sent as 'Authorization: Token YOUR_API_KEY' (or a token parameter). One key authenticates both the Data API and Project API.
access_notes: Fetched official 'Getting API access' page: all current paid plans (Essential, Pro, Business, Core, Growth, Enterprise) include Data API access with monthly credit allowances (10k-100k); keys are self-generated from the API Dashboard once subscribed. No free API tier documented.
api_surface_summary: Two REST APIs: Data API (keyword/SERP data, backlinks, website audit, etc., credit-metered) and Project API (manage projects/rankings against subscription limits). Rate limit 5 req/s.
main_blocker: API credentials require an active paid SE Ranking subscription; no free developer tier.
agent_notes: Docs are open and clear; official MCP page exists in their API docs. Once a customer has any paid plan the integration itself is straightforward key-based auth, so this is buildable for paying users.

one_line_description: SEO toolset whose API v3 exposes backlink, keyword, rank-tracking, and site-audit data.
auth_summary: API keys are created in Account settings (workspace owners/admins only, up to 1,000 keys, 1-year lifetime) and sent as 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY'.
access_notes: Historically API v3 was Enterprise-only; multiple 2026 sources report API access with monthly API-unit allocations now included on Lite/Standard/Advanced paid plans (Starter and free excluded). Either way, a paid subscription is required to obtain a key.
api_surface_summary: REST API v3 at docs.ahrefs.com covering the core Ahrefs dataset (Site Explorer/backlinks, keywords, rank tracker, site audit, management endpoints), metered in API units.
main_blocker: API keys are gated behind a paid Ahrefs subscription (formerly Enterprise-only; now reportedly Lite+), and usage consumes paid API units.
agent_notes: Auth mechanics confirmed from official docs via search. The exact plan threshold is in flux: official help center still frames API v3 around Enterprise, while 2026 third-party reports say all paid plans (Lite+) now get API units. Did not fetch the official pricing/plan matrix directly, so plan-gating details carry some uncertainty. Official Ahrefs MCP exists per their help center.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: AI-powered web scraping service with proxies and an API that extracts structured data from URLs via prompts.
auth_summary: API token obtained from the app Playground, passed via 'x-api-token' header (docs also show 'Authorization: Bearer' and a token query parameter).
access_notes: Self-serve: create a MrScraper account and grab the API key from the Playground at app.mrscraper.com/playground. Free-trial/free-tier specifics are not clearly documented; usage is credit-based.
api_surface_summary: REST API at api.mrscraper.com: prompt-based AI scraping endpoints plus scrapers/extractors and vertical scrapers (e.g., Google), with n8n and similar integrations documented.
agent_notes: Fetched the official quickstart, which confirms REST + x-api-token auth and self-serve key from the Playground. No official or community MrScraper MCP server found (only a third-party Apify actor that uses the mrscraper engine internally). Pricing/free-tier details and full endpoint breadth not fully confirmed.

one_line_description: Cloud platform for running web scraping and automation 'Actors', with thousands of prebuilt scrapers in its marketplace.
auth_summary: Personal API token from the Apify Console Integrations page, sent in the Authorization header (recommended) or as a ?token= query parameter; tokens support scoped permissions and expiration dates.
access_notes: Self-serve signup at console.apify.com; API token is available immediately, and a free plan with monthly platform credits exists (paid plans scale usage).
api_surface_summary: REST API v2 covering the whole platform: run/manage Actors, datasets, key-value stores, request queues, schedules, webhooks, users — plus per-Actor API endpoints and JS/Python API clients.
agent_notes: One of the easiest builds in this set: self-serve token, broad documented REST API, official MCP server documented in Apify's own platform docs. Actor runs consume platform credits, but a free tier exists.

one_line_description: API to scrape, crawl, map, search, and extract structured data from websites in LLM-ready formats.
auth_summary: API key (prefix 'fc-') from the Firecrawl dashboard, sent as 'Authorization: Bearer fc-YOUR-API-KEY'.
access_notes: Self-serve signup at firecrawl.dev; API key visible in the dashboard immediately, with a free credit tier and paid credit plans. Also open source (AGPL core) and self-hostable.
api_surface_summary: Focused REST API (v2): /scrape, /crawl, /map, /search, /extract endpoints returning markdown/structured JSON, plus official SDKs. Deep in scraping but a deliberately narrow domain.
agent_notes: Official MCP server maintained under the firecrawl GitHub org. Auth and endpoints confirmed from official docs domain via search; very standard Bearer API-key model. Usage consumes credits beyond the free tier.

one_line_description: Web data platform offering proxies, Web Unlocker, SERP API, scraper APIs, and prebuilt datasets.
auth_summary: API key (secure hashed token, auto-created on account creation; admins can mint more with permission levels and expiry) sent as a Bearer token for REST APIs. Separately, native proxy access uses proxy username/password credentials rather than the API key.
access_notes: Self-serve signup; a default API key is created automatically and keys are managed in account settings. Pay-as-you-go pricing per product; the official MCP has a free tier (~5,000 credits/month, no credit card). Some products (e.g., residential proxies) involve KYC/compliance steps.
api_surface_summary: Broad REST API surface at docs.brightdata.com: Web Unlocker, SERP API, Web Scraper APIs, dataset/marketplace endpoints, and proxy/account management.
agent_notes: Official vendor MCP (@brightdata/mcp on npm) with a genuinely free monthly tier. The product line is large, so a Composio toolkit would need scoping (Unlocker + SERP + scraper APIs are the natural core). Proxy user/pass auth is separate from the API-key path.

one_line_description: Open-source Python CLI that hunts for a username across 400+ social networks by probing public profile URLs.
auth_summary: No authentication at all: it is a local CLI tool, not a SaaS. It requires no API keys or logins for itself or for the sites it checks — it just requests public profile URLs and inspects responses.
access_notes: Free and open source (pipx/pip install sherlock-project, git clone, or the official Docker image). No account, credentials, or vendor relationship involved.
api_surface_summary: Single-purpose CLI: given username(s), it checks 400+ sites and reports where the handle exists, with flags for timeouts, output files, NSFW inclusion, etc. No hosted API of any kind.
main_blocker: No hosted API — Composio would have to run the Python CLI in its own execution environment and parse CLI output; results depend on brittle site-detection heuristics.
agent_notes: Recorded honestly as an OSS CLI, not a SaaS. Several community MCP wrappers exist (Burnsedia/sherlock-mcp, qKitNp/sherlock_mcp, p1atdev/sherlock-mcp); none are official. Note: fewsats/sherlock-mcp is an unrelated 'Sherlock Domains' product. A wrapped toolkit is feasible but requires self-hosting the tool and accepting slow multi-site scans and false positives/negatives.

one_line_description: B2B data waterfall API aggregating 30+ vendors for contact/company enrichment, email verification, phone numbers, prospecting, and job-change tracking.
auth_summary: API key passed in an 'x-api-key' header on every request, confirmed in the official docs at docs.waterfall.io.
access_notes: Public docs exist (docs.waterfall.io/v1/introduction) but neither the docs nor the homepage state how keys are issued; the site funnels visitors to 'Book a Call'. A third-party review claims every user gets an API key on signup, but I could not verify a self-serve signup flow from official sources.
api_surface_summary: REST API at api.waterfall.io with ~13 endpoints: prospector, contact/company search, contact/company/phone enrichment, email verification, job-change detection, and account/usage reporting; mixes sync and async (webhook-result) operations.
main_blocker: Credential acquisition path is unverified — marketing funnels to a demo call, and pricing/credit terms are not public.
agent_notes: Docs are real and reasonably complete (fetched the introduction page: REST, x-api-key, 13 endpoints, llms.txt index), which is better than expected for a thin-docs candidate. Main gaps: no public pricing, no confirmed self-serve signup, no MCP found. Cargo's integration docs reference an 'x-waterfall-api-key' header variant for their connector.

one_line_description: GTM data enrichment and workflow platform that orchestrates 150+ data providers in spreadsheet-style tables.
auth_summary: No general public API auth scheme is documented. Inbound webhooks support an optional auth token header; the Enterprise-only People & Company lookup API and the MCP integration are key/account-based (MCP connects via a guided login in Claude/ChatGPT connectors).
access_notes: Clay itself is self-serve SaaS, but per Clay's own docs/community answers it 'doesn't offer traditional API endpoints': developers get data in via self-serve webhooks and out via the in-table HTTP API action, while the direct People & Company enrichment API is restricted to Enterprise customers.
api_surface_summary: Generally available surface is webhook ingestion into tables (50k submissions per webhook) plus an outbound HTTP API action from tables; a proper REST lookup API (people/company) exists only for Enterprise plans.
main_blocker: No general-purpose public API — programmatic enrichment access is gated to Enterprise; everything else is webhook-in/HTTP-out around Clay tables.
agent_notes: Fetched clay.com/guides/clay-mcp: Clay has an official MCP exposing 150+ providers and workflows inside ChatGPT/Claude/Codex (500 free credits on first connect), which is arguably the sanctioned agent path in lieu of a public API. Caution: search results also surfaced an unrelated 'Clay' personal CRM (clay.earth, mcp.clay.earth, clay-inc/clay-mcp on GitHub) — do not conflate the two. Enterprise API details (auth header, endpoints) are not publicly documented.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Code hosting and collaboration platform for git repositories, issues, pull requests, and CI/CD (Actions).
auth_summary: REST API accepts fine-grained or classic personal access tokens, OAuth app tokens (OAuth2 authorization code flow), and GitHub App auth (app JWT to mint installation tokens, or user-to-server ghu_ tokens). All are sent as Authorization bearer/token headers.
access_notes: Any free GitHub account can create a PAT in settings instantly; OAuth apps and GitHub Apps are also self-registered from account/org settings with no approval step.
api_surface_summary: REST API covers essentially the whole platform (repos, issues, PRs, Actions, orgs, users, security alerts, projects), plus a full GraphQL v4 API.
agent_notes: Gold-standard integration target. Official MCP server is available both remote-hosted (api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ with OAuth) and local via Docker. Composio-style toolkits are straightforward; only nuance is choosing between PAT, OAuth app, and GitHub App installation auth per use case.

one_line_description: Frontend cloud platform for deploying and hosting web apps (Next.js et al.) with serverless functions, domains, and edge network.
auth_summary: All REST API calls to api.vercel.com authenticate with a Vercel Access Token created in account settings, sent as 'Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>'; tokens can be scoped to specific teams. Docs also show creating auth tokens programmatically and an SSO token exchange endpoint.
access_notes: Free Hobby accounts can generate access tokens immediately from account settings (Settings > Tokens); no approval or paid plan needed for API access.
api_surface_summary: REST API under api.vercel.com covers deployments, projects, domains, DNS, environment variables, teams, logs, and auth token management.
agent_notes: Official hosted MCP at mcp.vercel.com is OAuth-based and was launched read-only with an allowlist of approved clients. No user-facing OAuth2 flow documented for the general REST API itself (token-based); integration/marketplace OAuth exists but was not verified here. Team-scoped resources need a teamId query param.
verification_notes: Manually verified in QA sample; no corrections needed.

one_line_description: Web hosting and deployment platform for static sites and serverless functions with build automation and edge services.
auth_methods: Personal access token; OAuth2; Bearer token
auth_summary: The Netlify API (open-API/REST) accepts personal access tokens generated in user settings, sent as 'Authorization: Bearer <token>'. Public/third-party integrations must use OAuth2 (authorize endpoint at app.netlify.com/authorize).
access_notes: Free-tier Netlify accounts can create personal access tokens under Applications > Personal access tokens instantly; OAuth apps are also self-registered.
api_surface_summary: REST API covers sites, deploys, builds, DNS/domains, environment variables, forms, and account/team management — the full hosting control plane, though narrower in scope than hyperscaler APIs.
agent_notes: Official MCP (@netlify/mcp) wraps both API and CLI capabilities (deploys, env vars, extensions). API is described by an OpenAPI spec, which makes toolkit generation easy.

one_line_description: CDN, DNS, security, and edge-compute platform (Workers, R2, KV) fronting a large share of the web.
auth_summary: Recommended auth is scoped API Tokens (user or account-owned, cfut_ prefix) sent as 'Authorization: Bearer'; the legacy Global API Key (X-Auth-Key + X-Auth-Email headers) is still supported. Origin CA keys exist for certificate endpoints.
access_notes: Free Cloudflare accounts can mint scoped API tokens from the dashboard immediately; tokens can also be created via API. No approval needed.
